Job did not speak the things here recorded by way of boasting, but in answer to the charge of hypocrisy. He understood the spiritual nature of God’s commandments, as reaching to the thoughts and intents of the heart. It is best to let your actions speak for you; but in some cases you owe it to yourself and to the cause of God solemnly to protest your innocence of the crimes of which you are falsely accused. The lusts of the flesh, and the love of the world, are two fatal rocks on which many people split; against these Job protests he was always careful to stand upon his guard. And God takes more exact notice of you than you do of yourself; let us therefore walk carefully. He carefully avoided all sinful means of getting wealth. He dreaded all forbidden profit as much as all forbidden pleasure. What we have in the world may be used with comfort, or lost with comfort, if honestly gotten. Without strict honesty and faithfulness in all your dealings, you can have no good evidence of true godliness. Yet how many professors are unable to abide this touchstone!

All the defilements of life come from a deceived heart. Lust is a fire in the soul: those that indulge it are said to burn. It consumes all that is good there, and lays the conscience waste. It kindles the fire of God’s wrath, which, if not quenched by the blood of Christ, will consume even to eternal destruction. It consumes the body; it consumes the substance. Burning lusts bring burning judgments. Job had a numerous household, and he managed it well. He considered that he had a Master in heaven; and as we are undone if God should be severe with us, we ought to be mild and gentle towards all with whom we have to do.

Job’s conscience gave testimony concerning his just and charitable behavior toward the poor. He speaks at length on this subject, because in this matter he was particularly accused. He was careful of all, and hurtful to none. Notice the principles by which Job was restrained from being uncharitable and unmerciful. He stood in awe of the Lord, as certainly against him if he should wrong the poor. Regard for worldly interests may restrain a man from actual crimes; but the grace of God alone can make him hate, dread, and shun sinful thoughts and desires.

Job protests 1. That he never set his heart upon the wealth of this world. How few prosperous professors can appeal to the Lord that they have not rejoiced because their gains were great! Through the determination to be rich, many ruin their souls, or pierce themselves with many sorrows. 2. He never was guilty of idolatry. The source of idolatry is in the heart, and it corrupts men, and provokes God to send judgments upon a nation. 3. He neither desired nor delighted in the hurt of the worst enemy he had. If others bear malice to you, that will not justify you in bearing malice to them. 4. He had never been unkind to strangers. Hospitality is a Christian duty.

Job clears himself from the charge of hypocrisy. We are reluctant to confess our faults, willing to excuse them, and to lay the blame upon others. But he who thus covers his sins shall not prosper. He speaks of his courage in what is good, as an evidence of his sincerity in it. When men get estates unjustly, they are justly deprived of comfort from them; it was sown wheat, but shall come up thistles. What men do not come honestly by will never do them any good. The words of Job are ended. They end with a bold assertion that, with respect to the accusation against his moral and religious character as the cause for his sufferings, he could appeal to God. But, however confident Job was, we shall see he was mistaken, chapter ; . Let us all judge ourselves; wherein we are guilty, let us seek forgiveness in that blood which cleanseth from all sin; and may the Lord have mercy upon us, and write his laws in our hearts!
